one_line_takeaway: InstruCoT fine-tunes LLMs on synthetic multi-region prompt-injection data paired with structured 3-stage chain-of-thought instruction analysis, and the CoT component alone accounts for large chunks of its defense-rate gains (e.g., +38.0/+33.4 pp over the no-CoT InSFT ablation on Behavior Deviation) without sacrificing utility (82.9% average Win Rate).
key_finding: Averaged across the four backbone LLMs and all attack scenarios within each dimension: InstruCoT reaches 92.5% average DR for Behavior Deviation, 98.0% for Privacy Leakage, and 90.9% for Harmful Output, outperforming baselines by 25.8-82.5 percentage points (Behavior Deviation), 6.7-47.2 pp (Privacy Leakage), and 7.4-34.5 pp (Harmful Output). Specific Table 3 numbers: Behavior Deviation AVG DR -- Clean 10.3%/12.4% (direct/indirect), ISE 60.5%/72.9%, MetaSec 51.0%/85.9%, IP 9.7%/12.3%, PromptArmor 51.7%/49.8%, InSFT (ablation without CoT) 53.5%/60.1%, InstruCoT 91.5%/93.4%. Privacy Leakage AVG DR (ShareGPT/Unnatural datasets): Clean 47.1%/54.5%, ISE 91.2%/91.3%, MetaSec 73.7%/58.9%, IP 83.1%/83.7%, PromptArmor 56.3%/60.2%, InSFT 78.6%/86.0%, InstruCoT 97.6%/98.4%. Harmful Output AVG DR across 13 categories: Clean 56.4%, ISE 67.8%, MetaSec 80.2%, IP 58.0%, PromptArmor 70.8%, InSFT 83.5%, InstruCoT 90.9%. The InstruCoT vs InSFT (CoT ablation) gap -- 38.0/33.4 pp on Behavior Deviation, 15.7 pp on Privacy Leakage -- is presented as direct evidence that the CoT reasoning component (not just the diverse data) drives the gains. Utility: InstruCoT achieves average Win Rate 82.9% across the four LLMs, a 1.5-11.4 pp improvement over baselines (i.e., no utility degradation from the safety fine-tuning). CoT-quality evaluation (Table 2, human-annotated): average F1 98.3% for Instruction Perception, average Precision 99.7% for Violation Comprehension, 99.3% for Response Projection, evaluated across 7 dataset/context-region combinations (e.g., Alpaca-Clean/Data: 100/100/100; SystemChat-Adv/PI: 98.5/96.2/97.3 P/R/F1; Ultrachat-Adv/Data+User+PI: 98.6/99.3/99.0). Table 1 shows InstruCoT is the only compared dataset with checkmarks on all four quality dimensions (Injection Diversity, Scenario Diversity, Instruction Complexity, Sample Validity); StruQ/SecAlign/Meta-SecAlign fail all three diversity/complexity dimensions and ISE achieves only 'Partial' on the first two.
core_contribution: The paper proposes InstruCoT, a model-enhancement defense against prompt injection (PI) attacks in LLM-integrated applications. It has two novel pieces built by the authors themselves: (1) a diverse synthetic-data generation pipeline that produces injected instructions across three threat scenarios (Behavior Deviation, Privacy Leakage, Harmful Output) and inserts them into four context regions (User, Data, User+Data, Empty) derived from a dataflow-analysis-inspired reduction of real LLM application architectures; and (2) an instruction-level chain-of-thought (CoT) annotation scheme, modeled on Endsley's Situation Awareness theory (perception, comprehension, projection), that is appended to training targets so a fine-tuned LLM first reasons about each instruction in context before answering. The backbone LLM is then fully fine-tuned (supervised, next-token NLL loss) on this CoT-augmented dataset to learn to identify and refuse instructions that conflict with the system prompt, regardless of injection source or position.
threat_model: Adversaries inject malicious instructions into an LLM's context either directly (attacker-controlled user input) or indirectly (via manipulated external/tool/retrieved data that the LLM consumes). The paper frames three top-level attacker goals it studies: Behavior Deviation (steering the model away from its system-prompt-defined task), Privacy Leakage (extracting user PII, organizational confidential data, or system-prompt/system-level secrets), and Harmful Output (coercing generation of harmful content across 13 categories such as illegal activity, hate speech, malware, fraud, etc., following Shen et al. 2024's taxonomy). Attacks are evaluated via 7 concrete PI methods split into semantically-disjoint injection (Naive, Ignore, Escape-Character, Fake Completion, Combined, and their multi-position variants) and semantically-blurring injection (TopicAttack, which embeds the payload inside topically-related dialogue to erase the boundary between injected and legitimate content). The core assumption motivating the design is that (a) injection can occur through any of several context regions (User, Data, User+Data, Empty) and (b) injected text is not always lexically or positionally distinguishable from legitimate content, so role/position-based defenses (e.g., structured-query or instruction-hierarchy approaches) are insufficient.
limitations: The authors state two limitations explicitly: (1) InstruCoT incurs additional inference overhead relative to baselines because the fine-tuned model must generate CoT reasoning before producing its final response; they suggest speculative decoding, CoT distillation into implicit reasoning, or selectively triggering CoT via a lightweight classifier as possible mitigations, but do not implement or measure any of these mitigations or report actual latency/throughput numbers. (2) Training and test sets share the same three threat dimensions (Behavior Deviation, Privacy Leakage, Harmful Output) -- although train/test instruction content does not overlap, the paper has not demonstrated generalization to threat categories outside these three, and the authors flag exploring additional threat dimensions as future work. Additionally (not stated as a limitation by the authors but evident from the paper): all evaluated backbones are 7-8B parameter open-source models, so scaling behavior to larger models is untested; the synthetic injection instructions and CoT annotations are themselves LLM-generated (GPT-4.1), introducing potential dataset generator bias/artifacts that a human-only-authored attack corpus would not have; and the dataset/code link given is an anonymous review repository, so full reproducibility could not be independently verified from the paper text alone.

datasets_used: Ultrachat-Decomposed (Wu et al. 2025) - clean seed data
Unnatural (Unnatural Instructions dataset) - privacy-leakage evaluation set

13-category harmful-output evaluation set based on Shen et al. 2024 taxonomy (Illegal Activity, Hate Speech, Malware Generation, Physical Harm, Economic Harm, Fraud, Pornography, Political Lobbying, Privacy Violation, Legal Opinion, Financial Advice, Health Consultation, Government Decision)
quantitative_results: InstruCoT average Defense Rate: 92.5% Behavior Deviation, 98.0% Privacy Leakage, 90.9% Harmful Output (averaged across 4 LLMs and all attacks per dimension)
InstruCoT outperforms baselines by 25.8-82.5 pp (Behavior Deviation), 6.7-47.2 pp (Privacy Leakage), 7.4-34.5 pp (Harmful Output)
Behavior Deviation AVG DR, direct/indirect: Clean 10.3%/12.4%, ISE 60.5%/72.9%, MetaSec 51.0%/85.9%, IP 9.7%/12.3%, PromptArmor 51.7%/49.8%, InSFT 53.5%/60.1%, InstruCoT 91.5%/93.4%
Privacy Leakage AVG DR, ShareGPT/Unnatural: Clean 47.1%/54.5%, ISE 91.2%/91.3%, MetaSec 73.7%/58.9%, IP 83.1%/83.7%, PromptArmor 56.3%/60.2%, InSFT 78.6%/86.0%, InstruCoT 97.6%/98.4%
Harmful Output AVG DR across 13 categories: Clean 56.4%, ISE 67.8%, MetaSec 80.2%, IP 58.0%, PromptArmor 70.8%, InSFT 83.5%, InstruCoT 90.9%
InstruCoT vs InSFT (CoT ablation) gap: +38.0 pp direct / +33.4 pp indirect Behavior Deviation; +15.7 pp Privacy Leakage
CoT quality (Table 2, human-annotated, averaged): Instruction Perception F1 98.3%, Violation Comprehension Precision 99.7%, Response Projection Precision 99.3%
Utility: InstruCoT average Win Rate 82.9% across 4 LLMs, 1.5-11.4 pp improvement over baselines
Against TopicAttack (semantically-blurring injection, hardest case), InstruCoT reaches 70.3% (direct) / 87.7% (indirect) DR vs ISE's 21.9% average DR mentioned in text for this attack
baselines_compared: PromptArmor (Shi et al. 2025) - detection-based, plug-and-play, no fine-tuning, uses GPT-4 as guardrail
ISE / Instructional Segment Embedding (Wu et al. 2025) - model-enhancement, instruction hierarchy
MetaSec / Meta SecAlign (Chen et al. 2025d/e) - model-enhancement
IP (Zhang et al. 2025) - model-enhancement, appends defensive prompt after system prompt
InSFT - InstruCoT's own ablation: same synthetic dataset, fine-tuned WITHOUT the CoT augmentation
Dataset-quality-only comparisons (Table 1, not DR benchmarks): StruQ (Chen et al. 2025b), SecAlign (Chen et al. 2025c), Meta-SecAlign
